title: Introduction to JavaScript

learningObjectives.text: Explain what javascript is using a new example and justify the result.
Explain javascript's role in the web using a new example and justify the result.
Explain where javascript runs using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: Introduction to JavaScript is part of the mental model learners need before making reliable implementation decisions. It belongs to the **Orientation and Foundations** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **What JavaScript is** → **JavaScript's role in the web** → **Where JavaScript runs**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: What JavaScript is
sections.body: JavaScript is a general-purpose programming language standardized through ECMAScript. It is dynamically typed, supports multiple programming styles, and treats functions as values. A host runtime supplies capabilities beyond the language itself.

sections.title: JavaScript's role in the web
sections.body: HTML describes structure, CSS describes presentation, and JavaScript expresses behavior and application logic. It responds to events, changes the document, communicates with servers, and coordinates state.

sections.title: Where JavaScript runs
sections.body: JavaScript runs inside an engine embedded in a host environment. Browsers expose the DOM, timers, networking, and storage; server runtimes expose different APIs. Language features are portable, but host APIs depend on the environment.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.source:
const platform = 'JS2Next';
console.log(`${platform} runs JavaScript in the browser.`);
sections.explanation: A value is created, interpolated into a string, and passed to a host-provided console API.

commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.
summary: Introduction to JavaScript connects What JavaScript is, JavaScript's role in the web, Where JavaScript runs.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains “What JavaScript is” in the context of Introduction to JavaScript?

choices.label: JavaScript is a general-purpose programming language standardized through ECMAScript. It is dynamically typed, supports multiple programming styles, and treats functions as values. A host runtime supplies…
choices.feedback: What JavaScript is is best understood as follows: JavaScript is a general-purpose programming language standardized through ECMAScript. It is dynamically typed, supports multiple programming styles, and treats functions as values. A host runtime supplies…

choices.label: In object.method(), the object before the dot provides this for that call. Detaching the method changes the call form and may lose the intended receiver.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of What JavaScript is.

choices.label: Variables are not permanently bound to one type. Each value still has a definite runtime type, but a variable may refer to different kinds of values over time. Clear data boundaries and validation are therefore…
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of What JavaScript is.

choices.label: Array filter returns a new array containing only elements whose predicate is truthy.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of What JavaScript is.

questions.explanation: What JavaScript is is best understood as follows: JavaScript is a general-purpose programming language standardized through ECMAScript. It is dynamically typed, supports multiple programming styles, and treats functions as values. A host runtime supplies…
questions.hint: Focus on what the concept changes, enables, or constrains—not only on its syntax.

questions.prompt: A learner must use Introduction to JavaScript in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Introduction to JavaScript.

choices.label: Apply What JavaScript is deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Introduction to JavaScript.

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Introduction to JavaScript.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.

questions.prompt: What is the most accurate explanation of the following Introduction to JavaScript example?

choices.label: The snippet guarantees that all values are immutable and all operations are asynchronous.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Introduction to JavaScript.

choices.label: The snippet is valid only because JavaScript ignores variable scope and evaluation order.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Introduction to JavaScript.

choices.label: The snippet removes the need to understand the data flowing through the program.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Introduction to JavaScript.

choices.label: A value is created, interpolated into a string, and passed to a host-provided console API.
choices.feedback: The correct choice connects the example to the underlying model rather than treating the syntax as isolated trivia.

questions.explanation: The correct choice connects the example to the underlying model rather than treating the syntax as isolated trivia.
questions.hint: Trace data, control flow, ownership, or rendering step by step.

code.source:
const platform = 'JS2Next';
console.log(`${platform} runs JavaScript in the browser.`);

questions.prompt: Which response best addresses this common mistake: “Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.”?

choices.label: The statement is correct only when variable names are short.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Introduction to JavaScript.

choices.label: The statement is unrelated to program behavior and cannot cause defects.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Introduction to JavaScript.

choices.label: The statement is a misconception. A better approach is to reason from the concept's inputs, rules, and observable outcome, then verify the result.
choices.feedback: Good feedback names the misconception and replaces it with a usable reasoning process.

choices.label: The statement is always correct, so no further reasoning about Introduction to JavaScript is needed.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Introduction to JavaScript.

questions.explanation: Good feedback names the misconception and replaces it with a usable reasoning process.
questions.hint: Look for the option that corrects both the belief and the learner's next action.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Introduction to JavaScript?

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Introduction to JavaScript.

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Introduction to JavaScript.

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from What JavaScript is, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Introduction to JavaScript.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Introduction to JavaScript?

choices.label: Use Introduction to JavaScript when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Introduction to JavaScript.

choices.label: Avoid Introduction to JavaScript completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Introduction to JavaScript.

choices.label: Use Introduction to JavaScript in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Introduction to JavaScript.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
